title: Linux Command Line
theory: In Linux, everything is structured inside a single directory tree starting from the root directory `/`. The user interacts with the system using commands typed into the terminal shell (typically Bash). Commands follow the format `command -options arguments`. You navigate the tree using `cd`, locate files using `ls`, and perform file operations using `touch`, `mkdir`, `cp`, `mv`, and `rm`. Redirects like `>` and `>>` write command output to files.

interactive_examples.description: Prints your active username in the shell.
Displays the absolute path of your current folder.
Lists all directory files including hidden files starting with a dot.
Prints the complete content of welcome.txt directly to output.

exercises.description: Navigate to `/etc` and list all files using long formatting: `cd /etc && ls -l`.
Read the hidden shell configuration file `.bashrc` in your home directory: `cat ~/.bashrc`.
Create a nested folders structure under home: `mkdir -p workspace/testing`.

quiz.question: Which command outputs the current folder path?
Which flag allows listing hidden files starting with a dot?
What is the tilde (~) symbol a shortcut for?

lessons.title: 1. Terminal Introduction
lessons.definition: The terminal is a text-based interface used to control a computer.
lessons.explanation: Instead of clicking icons, you type commands. The system executes them and displays results.
lessons.instruction: Identify your logged-in username by executing the whoami command.

lessons.title: 2. Locate Directory (pwd)
lessons.definition: In Linux, your current location in the directory tree is the working directory.
lessons.explanation: The pwd (print working directory) command prints your absolute path from the root directory.
lessons.instruction: Check the current directory path in the terminal.

lessons.title: 3. List Files (ls)
lessons.definition: The ls command lists the files and directories inside your current folder.
lessons.explanation: By default, it shows visible files. You will see directories like drafts and welcome.txt.
lessons.instruction: List directory contents using the ls command.

lessons.title: 4. List Hidden Files (ls -a)
lessons.definition: Hidden files in Linux start with a dot (.) and contain configurations.
lessons.explanation: Passing -a to ls tells it to list all files, including hidden ones like .bashrc.
lessons.instruction: List all contents, including hidden configurations.

lessons.title: 5. List Details (ls -l)
lessons.definition: Long listing format displays permissions, owner, size, and modification date.
lessons.explanation: Using ls -l outputs detailed parameters of files in a table layout.
lessons.instruction: Run ls with the -l flag to view detailed file info.

lessons.title: 6. Navigating Folders (cd)
lessons.definition: The cd command changes your working directory location.
lessons.explanation: Specify the directory name to move inside. For example, cd drafts enters the drafts folder.
lessons.instruction: Change your active directory to drafts.

lessons.title: 7. Go Home (cd ~)
lessons.definition: The tilde symbol (~) represents your user's home directory path.
lessons.explanation: Running cd with ~ or no arguments instantly returns you to /home/student.
lessons.instruction: Navigate back to your home directory.

lessons.title: 8. Create Files (touch)
lessons.definition: The touch command creates a new empty file.
lessons.explanation: If the file already exists, touch updates its last-modified timestamp.
lessons.instruction: Create a file named draft.txt in the current directory.

lessons.title: 9. Create Folders (mkdir)
lessons.definition: The mkdir command creates a new directory folder.
lessons.explanation: This allows you to organize files in subfolders. Use mkdir projects to create a folder.

lessons.title: 10. Write File (echo & >)
lessons.definition: The > operator redirects output to a file, overwriting its contents.
lessons.explanation: echo 'text' > file writes 'text' into the file. If the file doesn't exist, it is created.
lessons.instruction: Write the string 'Hello DevLab' into draft.txt.

lessons.title: 11. Append File (echo & >>)
lessons.definition: The >> operator appends content to the end of a file.
lessons.explanation: Unlike >, >> keeps the existing content of the file and adds new text below it.

lessons.title: 12. View File (cat)
lessons.definition: The cat command concatenates and displays the contents of a file.
lessons.explanation: It prints the entire file content directly to your terminal standard output.

lessons.title: 13. Copy File (cp)
lessons.definition: The cp command duplicates files or directories.
lessons.explanation: Specify the source file followed by the destination path.

lessons.title: 14. Move File (mv)
lessons.definition: The mv command moves or renames a file.
lessons.explanation: Moving relocates the file, removing it from the original path.
lessons.instruction: Move draft.txt into the projects directory.

lessons.title: 15. Delete File (rm)
lessons.explanation: Warning: files deleted with rm cannot be recovered in Linux!
